facts.label: ASIAN ITALY
facts.story: Uzbekistan's players are the White Wolves, but abroad they've earned a stranger nickname: Osiyo Italiyasi, the 'Asian Italy.' It's description, not flattery. The white shirts and blue accents echo the Azzurri, and so does the football: patient, compact, willing to sit deep and strangle a game rather than chase it. That defensive identity was forged through three decades of Asian qualifying heartbreak — a controversial 2006 exit to Bahrain, a penalty shootout loss to Jordan in 2013, and back-to-back near misses in 2018 and 2022.

facts.label: BORN IN THE USSR
facts.story: Long before independence, there was the Uzbekistan Soviet Socialist Republic side, one of six core football centers of the USSR alongside Russia, Ukraine, and Georgia. In 1928, wearing the republic's colors at the Spartakiade, the team played its first-ever international match — against Switzerland — and won 8–4. That result stood as a high point for decades: Uzbekistan didn't take the field as an independent nation until June 1992, a 2–2 draw with Tajikistan in Dushanbe, months after the Soviet Union that had once fielded them ceased to exist.
